headline: Anchored attention, neutral affect — the pure-scroll signature.
synthesis_paragraph: A 30-second Instagram scroll plays through four visually unrelated cuts — a hand opening a snack, an animated cookie-stand, a woman raising her phone for a photo, a static reaction meme — and the trace reads exactly like passive feed-watching. The visual network sustains dominance across nearly the entire clip, peaking around t=4-5s and again at t=23-24s where the cuts get denser. The default-mode network stays suppressed throughout, suggesting attention is anchored outward at the screen rather than drifting into self-referential thought. The limbic specialist holds at a slight negative baseline — the clip is emotionally neutral, the kind of content that gets watched once and scrolled past. The closing second tells a small but specific story: ventral-attention takes the top slot for the first time in the clip, suggesting the static meme triggered a brief salience response, the perceptual jolt of switching from motion to a still frame.

temporal_arc.description: Visual onset; attention anchors to the snack-packet open.
Sustained visual dominance across the cookie-stand and photo-framing cuts; DMN stays suppressed.
Second visual peak on the densest cut sequence; limbic stays flat — engagement without investment.
Brief somatomotor flicker, then ventral-attention spike on the static meme close-out — a small salience event.

neural_evidence: Visual network peaks at 0.36 (t=4-5s and t=23-24s).
Default-mode network stays at 0.06 peak — strongly suppressed throughout.
Limbic network holds at 0.006 peak — essentially flat.
Ventral-attention takes top region only at t=29s (the meme close-out).
inflection_moment: t=29s — the first frame where ventral-attention overtakes visual, marking the shift from steady scroll to a brief novelty-detection response on the static meme.
